chunk_summary: This section discusses therapeutic iron dosing, monitoring, and evaluating non-response in pediatric anemia, emphasizing adherence, dose adjustments, and addressing underlying causes like malabsorption or infections.
learning_objectives: Understand therapeutic iron dosing guidelines for pediatric anemia, including dosage ranges and administration tips.
Learn how to monitor treatment response, including expected timelines for reticulocyte and hemoglobin changes.
Identify common causes of non-response to iron therapy and appropriate troubleshooting steps.
Recognize the role of deworming and dietary interventions in preventing anemia relapse.
key_facts: Oral elemental iron is prescribed at 3–6 mg/kg/day, starting at 3 mg/kg/day to improve adherence.
Reticulocyte response is expected within 1 week, and hemoglobin should rise within 2–4 weeks.
Therapeutic iron should continue for at least 3 months after hemoglobin normalization to replete iron stores.
Non-response may be due to poor adherence, hemoglobinopathies, chronic inflammation, or malabsorption.
Severe anemia in malnourished children requires careful escalation, with transfusion reserved for decompensation.
reference_quote: Oral elemental iron is typically prescribed at 3–6 mg/kg/day; many pediatric guidelines support starting at the lower end (around 3 mg/kg/day) to improve adherence and then adjusting based on response and tolerability. (Source: https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-059-prevention-of-iron-deficiency-anemia.pdf)
document_key_topics: Therapeutic iron dosing in pediatric anemia
Monitoring treatment response and troubleshooting non-response
Addressing underlying causes of anemia, including malabsorption and infections
Integration of deworming and dietary interventions in anemia care
